Feature Description (brief): Stream automatically surfaces tasks from your visit — follow-ups, reminders, actions — and organizes them in a central task list for your practice. Resolve them manually and they’ll carry forward until completed.
Feature Image Alt Text: Illustration of Stream listening to a clinician and automatically generating a task list from the encounter.
Hero Subheading: Let Stream listen, extract actionable items, and organize what needs to happen next — so you never lose track of follow-ups or important clinical steps.
Long Body:
In clinical care, the hard part isn’t always deciding what to do — it’s making sure it actually gets done. Between follow-up steps, administrative tasks, care coordination, and the realities of visit-to-visit documentation, important clinical details can easily slip through the cracks.

Stream’s Task Management feature eliminates that cognitive burden.

Stream listens and identifies actionable items automatically

Whether you’re speaking naturally, typing into the Scratchpad, or documenting through templates, Stream identifies and surfaces the next steps implied by your visit — follow-ups to schedule, instructions to carry out, reminders to address at the next visit, or actions to complete outside the room.

There’s no setup required. No triggers to define. No special syntax.

If you say it, Stream captures it.


A unified task list for your practice

Stream gathers all identified tasks in a dedicated Task Manager page, filterable by provider. This gives your entire practice a single source of truth for clinical and administrative follow-ups — a view that’s far easier to manage than a mix of sticky notes, inbox messages, or EHR reminders.


Tasks carry forward until you complete them

Every task persists across encounters until you mark it resolved. If you don’t complete the task today, it will still appear the next time you see the patient.

This reduces the mental overhead of remembering what needs to happen at future visits.

It also prevents important gaps in follow-up care.


Simple resolution, clean workflows

Tasks are manually resolved with a single action. No automatic clearing, no hidden logic — you maintain full control.


Designed for Stream Pro

Tasks depend on Stream’s longitudinal memory, context loading, and continuity model. Because of this, Task Management is available exclusively in Stream Pro.

Feature Description (brief): Quick Encounter — Fast Notes When Context Isn’t Needed
Feature Image Alt Text: Clinician speaks to AI scribe in Quick Encounter and documentation is done in an instant.
H1 (Hero Heading): Quick Encounter — Fast Notes When Context Isn’t Needed
Hero Subheading: Use Stream’s lightweight mode for urgent visits, telemedicine, and short encounters where continuity doesn’t matter — and speed does.
Long Body:
Not every encounter needs Stream’s full memory.

Sometimes, you just need the note done — quickly, clearly, and once.

Quick Encounter is Stream’s built-in “Lite Mode.” It’s powered by the same voice capture and transcription intelligence as Stream, but built for single encounters where long-term context or problem continuity aren’t required.

Use Quick Encounter to dictate, transcribe, or instruct — and get structured notes instantly. No setup, no configuration, no carryover.

It’s ideal for:

  • Urgent care or walk-in visits

  • Brief telemedicine encounters

  • Procedural follow-ups

  • One-time specialist consultations

Each note stands on its own, using your template of choice — but still benefits from Stream’s accuracy and structure.

Key Benefits

  • Fast Documentation: Capture your encounter naturally. Quick Encounter delivers finished notes in seconds.

  • Natural Input: Speak, dictate, or type. Stream interprets and structures your words automatically.

  • Custom Templates: Use any specialty- or visit-specific template — the same ones you already use in Stream.

  • No Carryover Context: Notes are self-contained, ideal for visits that don’t require continuity or follow-up.

Why It Matters

Even within a continuity-based system, clinicians often balance two realities:

  • Some visits build on months of context.

  • Others are quick, isolated, and transactional.

Quick Encounter allows Stream to flex between these modes seamlessly — giving you full power when you need context, and pure speed when you don’t.

This duality means Stream adapts to your workflow, not the other way around.
SEO Title: Quick, flexible AI medical scribe to match your urgent care and televisit documentation needs.

FAQ Q1: How is Quick Encounter different from Stream Pro?
FAQ A1: Quick Encounter is Stream’s lightweight documentation mode designed for single visits that don’t require longitudinal tracking or memory. It uses the same transcription and structured note generation engine as Stream Pro but skips context loading — making it faster for urgent care, telehealth, or one-time consultations.
FAQ Q2: Can I still use my custom templates with Quick Encounter?
FAQ A2: Yes. Quick Encounter supports custom, unstructured templates that reflect the entirety of the visit. You can dictate naturally or type notes into your preferred format — SOAP, narrative, or specialty-based — and Stream will generate a complete note using your chosen layout or custom templated instructions.
FAQ Q3: When should I use Quick Encounter instead of Stream Pro?
FAQ A3: Use Quick Encounter when continuity isn’t essential — for example, short-term or episodic visits like acute illness, or urgent care. Stream Pro is best for longitudinal care, where memory and prior context improve accuracy and efficiency over time.

Feature Description (brief): Create, edit, and reuse templates across every note style—from problem-based SOAP sections to encounter-level notes. Stream adapts to your preferred documentation workflow.

H1 (Hero Heading): Templates That Work the Way You Do
Hero Subheading: Stream lets you design templates that fit your specialty, visit type, and preferred chart structure—from single-problem SOAP notes to full encounter summaries.
Long Body:
Stream’s template system gives you control without friction.

You can design templates for any part of the documentation process: 

• Subjective – customize how patient narratives appear, whether bullet-pointed symptoms or structured HPI subsections.

• Objective – capture exams, vitals, labs, or imaging details with reusable formatting.

• Assessment & Plan – standardize your reasoning, differential language, and management plans for consistency across visits. 

For clinicians who prefer problem-oriented documentation, Stream also supports full problem-based templates—SOAP cards written entirely about one medical issue. Each card can inherit its own structure and style.

You can move seamlessly along the spectrum—from fully problem-based notes (SOAP per problem) to encounter-based notes (S/O/A grouped by visit). Even in encounter mode, Stream can still apply problem-specific mini-templates, ensuring consistent organization and carry-forward structure.

Templates are reusable, editable, and stored securely inside your workspace. Once you’ve created one, simply select it—or let Stream auto-apply it based on visit type or specialty context.

The result: documentation that’s faster, cleaner, and consistently reflects how you think.

Feature Description (brief): Real-time voice capture that builds structured, problem-based notes automatically.

H1 (Hero Heading): Transcribe, Dictate, Instruct — Your Voice as Prompt & Context
Hero Subheading: Speak naturally. Stream transcribes and structures your note in real time — your voice doubles as the prompt and the context.
Long Body:
You can work with Stream the way you work best — by speaking, typing, or both.

Whether you prefer to dictate naturally or give direct instructions, Stream turns your input into structured, problem-based documentation automatically. There are no rigid commands or templates to memorize — just a conversation that turns into a note.

You might say:

“Add this under the asthma plan, not the pneumonia plan.”

“Document everything under mild asthma exacerbation.”

“Draft a message I can send to the patient detailing inhaler use and put it under the plan.”

Stream listens and acts. It places information exactly where it belongs, at the right level of detail, under the right problem.

You can also type into Stream’s scratchpad if you prefer text-based input. Drop in your notes, lab results, or updates — Stream understands both the content and your intent. Write instructions like:

“Add this to diabetes: A1C from today is 7.4.”

Stream doesn’t just transcribe; it interprets and structures.

And if you use templates, you can instantly pull in reusable clinical instructions tailored to the context — for example, your preferred documentation pattern for chronic care visits or asthma follow-ups.

There’s no limit to what you can say or write.

Your voice, your notes, your system — Stream adapts to your practice.

H1 (Hero Heading): Collaborative Threads — Keep Everyone on the Same Page
Hero Subheading: Lightweight discussion around problems and encounters — linked to the chart, not buried in email.
Long Body:
In Stream, documentation isn’t owned by a single author — it’s a shared thread of care.

When multiple clinicians care for the same patient, the story shouldn’t reset with every encounter. Stream allows collaboration at the level that matters most: the medical problem. Each problem — whether it’s hypertension, anxiety, or back pain — becomes its own living thread that can be updated, expanded, or clarified by anyone on the care team.

This means a clinician seeing the patient today doesn’t just review the last note — they continue the same clinical thread.

The most recent assessment and plan remain visible and editable, so context is never lost and the story always moves forward.

Whether a pharmacist updates medication management, a specialist refines the plan, or a primary care provider summarizes the overall trajectory, Stream ensures everyone contributes to the same, problem-based narrative — not a scattered set of disconnected notes.

Collaborative Threads turn documentation into a team sport: fluid, traceable, and organized around patient reality instead of provider silos.

When each clinician contributes to a shared record that remembers where the last left off, continuity becomes effortless — and collaboration becomes the default.

H1 (Hero Heading): Record Ingestion — Bring Outside Docs Into Order
Hero Subheading: Upload or forward records; Stream ingests, extracts problems, and prepares summaries or updates.
Long Body:
Every clinician knows the feeling — a new patient arrives with years of records from outside systems: scanned PDFs, faxed letters, printouts from old EHRs. You spend half the visit scrolling, searching, and summarizing before you can even begin to plan care.

Stream’s Record Ingestion feature is designed to fix that.

Simply upload a PDF or paste text from any outside record, and Stream automatically extracts the relevant information — problems, medications, key findings — and files each item into the right problem buckets for you.

Discharge summaries, referral letters, and old consult notes become structured, problem-linked documentation that you can actually use. The next time you see the patient, you’re not digging through attachments — you’re continuing the story from where someone else left off.

This feature will also form the foundation of Stream’s Chart Builder, allowing you to reconstruct a patient’s longitudinal record even when they come from multiple external systems.

And soon, you’ll even be able to fax directly into Stream — sending outside documents straight into the problem-based charting system for instant processing. (We’re gauging early interest in this — stay tuned.)

With Record Ingestion, Stream turns information overload into structured, usable context — bringing clarity back to every handoff, every referral, and every new patient you meet.

Feature Description (brief): Persistent context across encounters for smarter follow-ups and instant summaries.
Feature Image:alt: Stream timeline illustrating clinical continuity with Past, Recent, and Today visits connected by a blue line, leading to a Hypertension card showing organized sections for problems, medications, recent changes, and next steps.

H1 (Hero Heading): Clinical Context & Continuity — Memory Built In
Hero Subheading: Stream remembers what you shouldn’t have to: problems, meds, and changes across visits for faster reviews and better handoffs.
Long Body:
Every encounter builds on the last. Yet in most EHRs, every note feels like starting over — disconnected from what came before and unaware of what comes next.

Stream changes that by making continuity an invisible part of the workflow. Each medical problem — hypertension, asthma, diabetes, depression — carries its own thread of context across time. When you open a new note and select the problems to address, Stream automatically retrieves the relevant prior documentation for each one.

That means your “Hypertension” section today doesn’t begin with a blank space — it begins with memory.

Stream references your last documentation for that problem and uses it as context to help generate more complete, more accurate, and more consistent notes.

Instead of retyping old plans, you simply confirm or adjust what’s changed. Stream carries forward the right details — active meds, recent assessments, ongoing plans — while leaving out what’s outdated.

The result is a note that feels current and continuous, not copy-pasted.

This problem-based continuity also helps Stream learn your style. Because Stream can see how you phrased things before, your documentation stays consistent — across visits, across patients, across time.

And when you or a colleague review the chart, each problem reads as one living narrative:

a clear, concise, longitudinal record of care.

In medicine, context is everything. Stream keeps it where it belongs — right beside the problem itself.

Feature Description (brief): Automation for coding (ICD-10 & CPT), problem summaries, referrals, and letters — generated from the note with clinician oversight.
Feature Image Alt Text: Stream AI automation transforming a structured hypertension note into ICD and CPT codes, referral letters, and FMLA documentation, with HCC support coming soon.
Hero Subheading: Let Stream handle codes, letters, and forms so you can focus on care.
Long Body:
Every note you write in Stream already contains the information needed to complete the administrative work that follows. Instead of treating those downstream steps as afterthoughts, Stream automates them directly from your structured documentation — so you spend less time retyping and more time moving forward.

From each assessment and plan, Stream intelligently proposes ICD and CPT codes that match the problems you addressed. You can review, confirm, or adjust them instantly — without switching screens or scrolling through code libraries. Everything remains transparent and clinician-controlled: Stream recommends; you decide.

That same contextual intelligence powers referrals, letters, and forms.

Because every note in Stream is organized by problem, the system can generate problem-specific documents automatically — referral letters that summarize the relevant history and plan for asthma, or FMLA forms that capture the right details for back pain — all without copying and pasting.

Stream’s automation doesn’t mean loss of nuance; it means removing repetition. Each document still reflects your words and your judgment, only faster.

And soon, Stream will extend these capabilities with HCC recommendations and Task Management, creating a seamless bridge from documentation to next steps — follow-ups, reminders, and shared team tasks, all grounded in the same problem-based structure.

At its core, Stream handles the administrative weight of medicine the same way it handles documentation itself:

intelligently, transparently, and always under your command.

Long Body:
Every problem tells a story — and Stream keeps each one coherent.

In traditional EHRs, reviewing a patient means flipping through a stack of disconnected notes, trying to reconstruct the clinical narrative. In Stream, that story is already organized for you.

Each medical problem — hypertension, asthma, fatigue, depression — maintains its own longitudinal summary, built from the documentation you’ve already written. When you open a problem, you see its history at a glance: what changed, what’s ongoing, and what’s planned next.

These summaries are more than static text; they’re living overviews that update automatically as new encounters are created. You can scroll back through time by problem or view the same data encounter by encounter.

And because each problem summary reflects structured, problem-based documentation, Stream can instantly assemble a Patient Summary — a clear, unified snapshot of the entire patient, built from the most recent version of every active problem.

The result:

  • No more flipping between encounters to see progress.

  • No more confusion about what’s current versus outdated.

  • No more copying forward just to remember what changed.

With Stream, continuity doesn’t have to be recreated — it’s already there.

Whether reviewing a single problem or the whole patient, Stream gives you an immediate, accurate, and effortless overview of care.

Hero Subheading: Every encounter is organized by problem with linked A/P and updates — built for reviewability.
Long Body:
Every chart tells a story — but only if it’s organized by what actually matters: the patient’s medical problems.

Stream replaces the EHR’s encounter-centered chaos with a truly problem-based structure. Instead of forcing you to navigate pages of unstructured notes, Stream builds your documentation around clearly defined, clinician-controlled problems — like Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, or Anxiety Disorder.

During each encounter, Stream intelligently scans your transcript and scratchpad to identify the problems that were discussed. You review and confirm the list — editing names or combining issues however you prefer. “Hypertension” can stay simple, or you can specify “Essential Hypertension”; Stream respects your language.

Each confirmed title becomes part of the patient’s problem list, serving as both the foundation for today’s note and the structure for ongoing continuity.

ICD codes are automatically recommended from each problem title, but they’re secondary — organization is always clinical first, billing second. Multiple ICDs can attach to a single problem to simplify your workflow. For example, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with nephropathy and neuropathy can live comfortably under a single, unified problem — not scattered across two redundant sections.

Problems can later be merged or edited, and each one carries its own longitudinal history over time.

The result is a chart that reflects clinical reality: flexible, intuitive, and built to evolve with the patient.

Stream’s problem-based structure means you spend less time looking for information and more time understanding it.

It’s the foundation of Stream’s entire ecosystem — clarity, continuity, and context, all organized by problem.
